title: 93YaleLJ
description: Owen M. Fiss — Against Settlement (93 Yale L.J. 1073, 1984)
show_notes:
Owen M. Fiss — Against Settlement (93 Yale L.J. 1073, 1984)

Owen Fiss's classic 1984 critique of the Alternative Dispute Resolution movement. Against the prevailing view that settlement is a desirable, cheaper substitute for adjudication, Fiss argues that settlement is the civil analogue of plea bargaining — a capitulation to the conditions of mass society that should be neither encouraged nor praised. He marshals four objections: settlement is distorted by disparities in the parties' resources; it founders on the problem of who can give authoritative consent for organizations and groups; it provides no basis for the continuing judicial involvement that structural-reform and other cases demand; and it deprives courts of the occasion to interpret public values and give force to law. Adjudication, on his account, is not merely private dispute resolution but a public exercise of state power in service of constitutional and statutory ideals.
